content.text: What Eval Dataset Versioning Is

An eval dataset is the collection of test cases—prompts, expected outputs, scoring rubrics—that determine whether a language-model pipeline passes quality gates before it ships. Eval dataset versioning treats that collection as a first-class artifact with the same lifecycle discipline applied to application source code: every change is committed, every release is tagged, and the full history is auditable. Without versioning, two pipeline runs comparing different agent builds may be comparing against different test cases, making the numbers meaningless.

In practice, an eval dataset lives in a Git repository (or a subfolder of the application repo), with large binary case files tracked via DVC or Git LFS and metadata in plain YAML or JSONL. A semantic version tag on the dataset—independent of the application version—lets CI pipelines pin to an exact dataset snapshot. This separation matters: a dataset can evolve (adding edge cases, fixing mislabeled ground truth) without coupling to a particular model release.
Why It Matters

LLM-based systems have non-deterministic, distribution-shift failure modes that traditional unit tests cannot catch. The eval dataset is the empirical record of expected behavior. If the dataset itself changes without versioning, you lose the ability to distinguish three very different events: the model got better, the dataset got easier, or the threshold was lowered. Reproducibility collapses, and regressions sneak past CI undetected.

Promotion rules—thresholds the dataset-plus-model combination must meet before advancing through environments—encode the quality contract. A CI gate that promotes to staging only when pass rate exceeds 88% and no previously-passing case now fails gives teams a repeatable, reviewable definition of 'ready to ship.' Without versioned datasets underpinning those gates, the contract is unenforceable.
Benefits
Reproducible CI runs — pinning dataset version ensures the same 1,200 cases are scored on every run, so pass-rate changes are attributable to model or prompt changes only.
Auditable regressions — git log on the dataset repo shows exactly which cases were added or modified between releases, making regression investigation tractable.
Drift detection — automated diffing between dataset versions flags coverage drops, schema mismatches, and unexpected case removal before a PR merges.
Decoupled evolution — dataset maintainers can add cases for new features while model engineers iterate independently; the CI pipeline resolves the combination at run time.
Legal and compliance traceability — regulated industries can demonstrate which test cases validated a model release, linking artifact version to dataset version to results.
Trade-offs and Limitations

Storage overhead — JSONL case files with few thousand examples stay small, but datasets with images, audio, or multi-turn transcripts grow to gigabytes quickly; Git LFS or DVC adds operational complexity.
Two-repo coordination burden — application and dataset repos must be synchronized; a dataset schema breaking change requires a coordinated release across both repos.
Threshold drift — fixed numeric thresholds (pass rate >= 88%) become stale as datasets expand; a dataset with 10x more hard cases will produce lower scores even for an unchanged model.
Ground truth maintenance — labeled expected outputs become stale as the intended behavior of the product evolves; versioning helps but does not eliminate the relabeling work.
How It Works: Mechanics

The eval dataset lives in a Git repository as a directory tree. The manifest file declares the schema version, dataset semantic version, and pointers to case files. Large case files (JSONL, Parquet) are stored in DVC or Git LFS; only their content hashes are committed to Git. When a new dataset version is ready, a version tag is pushed (e.g., evals/v2.3.1). The CI pipeline reads the tag from a configuration file in the application repo and fetches exactly that dataset snapshot.

A dataset diff job runs between the pinned version and the proposed new version, categorizing changes into four buckets: added cases, removed cases, modified cases (same ID, changed content), and schema changes. Added and removed cases trigger coverage checks. Modified cases invalidate cached baseline scores for those IDs. Schema changes (a different field name or a new required field) trigger a major version bump and block auto-promotion until human review approves the change.
Semantic Versioning for Eval Datasets

Dataset semantic versioning mirrors SemVer but with domain-specific semantics. A MAJOR bump signals a breaking schema change—consumer code that reads the dataset must be updated. A MINOR bump adds new test cases without changing the schema; existing baselines remain valid. A PATCH bump corrects mislabeled ground truth, fixes typos, or removes duplicate cases; the schema and case count stay within tolerance.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Eval dataset versioning flow: dataset repo with DVC, CI trigger on tag push, eval harness scoring, promotion gate with pass rate threshold, and semantic versioning timeline

content.text: Mental Model: The Dataset as a Test Suite Tag

Think of the eval dataset like the test suite in a traditional software project, but the test suite itself is versioned and distributed as an artifact. When pytest runs in CI, the exact test files committed to the repo at that revision are what runs—not some mutable collection that someone edited yesterday. Eval dataset versioning enforces the same discipline: the CI pipeline is always told 'run test suite v2.3.1,' and that version is immutable. Changing a test case requires a new version tag.
When to Use It
Use it when multiple teams or pipelines consume the same eval cases—the version tag prevents one team's edits from silently affecting another's CI run.
Use it when you need to compare model versions against the exact same benchmark—pinned dataset versions make A/B results apples-to-apples.
Use it when operating in regulated environments requiring documented evidence of testing artifacts at each release.
Skip it for throwaway prototype evals with fewer than 50 cases that no production gate depends on—the overhead of DVC setup outweighs the benefit at that scale.
How to Implement: Step by Step
Create a dedicated evals repository (or an evals/ subdirectory). Initialize DVC with dvc init and configure a remote (S3, GCS, or Azure Blob).
Define a manifest.yaml at the root declaring schema_version, dataset_version, and file listings with expected row counts.
Track large JSONL files with dvc add cases/. and commit the resulting .dvc pointer files to Git.
Tag releases with git tag evals/v2.3.1 and push both the tag and DVC remote.
In the application CI workflow, read the pinned dataset version from eval_config.yaml, check out the dataset tag, and run dvc pull to materialize case files.
Add a dataset-diff job that compares the proposed new dataset version against the pinned version and fails if schema breaks or coverage drops below threshold.
Encode promotion rules in a gate job that reads eval results, checks pass_rate, regression_count, and coverage, and either advances the pipeline or fails with a structured error.
Example: Dataset Manifest

# evals/manifest.yaml
dataset_version: "2.3.1"
schema_version: 3
created_at: "2026-07-10T14:00:00Z"
description: "Agentic AI routing and tool-use eval suite"

files:
  - path: cases/routing.jsonl
    rows: 340
    sha256: a3f8c2...
  - path: cases/tool_use.jsonl
    rows: 512
    sha256: 91bd44...
  - path: cases/safety.jsonl
    rows: 248
    sha256: 7e12fa...

promotion_rules:
  min_pass_rate: 0.88
  max_regression_count: 0
  min_coverage_ratio: 0.95

schema_contract:
  required_fields: ["id", "prompt", "expected", "category", "difficulty"]
  optional_fields: ["reference_tool_calls", "judge_rubric"]
  allowed_categories: ["routing", "tool_use", "safety", "multi_step"]
Example: CI Workflow for Dataset Versioning

# .github/workflows/eval-ci.yml
name: Eval CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

env:
  EVAL_DATASET_VERSION: "2.3.1"

jobs:
  validate-dataset:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Checkout eval dataset at pinned version
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          repository: my-org/agentic-ai-evals
          ref: evals/v${{ env.EVAL_DATASET_VERSION }}
          path: evals
          token: ${{ secrets.EVALS_REPO_TOKEN }}

      - name: Pull DVC data
        working-directory: evals
        run: |
          pip install dvc[s3]
          dvc pull --no-run-cache

      - name: Validate dataset schema
        run: |
          python scripts/validate_dataset_schema.py \
            --manifest evals/manifest.yaml \
            --cases-dir evals/cases/

      - name: Run evals
        run: |
          python scripts/run_evals.py \
            --dataset-dir evals/cases/ \
            --model ${{ vars.EVAL_MODEL }} \
            --output results/eval_results.json

      - name: Check promotion gate
        run: |
          python scripts/check_promotion_gate.py \
            --results results/eval_results.json \
            --manifest evals/manifest.yaml
        # exits non-zero if pass_rate < 0.88 or any regression found

      - name: Upload results artifact
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results-${{ github.sha }}
          path: results/
Example: Promotion Gate Script
Real-World Example: Routing Agent Release

A team ships a routing agent that classifies customer support queries and selects a downstream tool. Their eval dataset v2.3.0 contains 1,100 labeled queries across four categories. When a prompt engineer rewrites the system prompt, the application repo PR triggers CI with dataset pinned to evals/v2.3.0. The eval harness scores all 1,100 cases. Pass rate comes back at 91%, above the 88% threshold. Zero previously-passing cases now fail. The promotion gate emits PASS and the PR merges to staging.

Two weeks later, the data team adds 200 new safety edge cases to the dataset and tags it evals/v2.4.0. The manifest MINOR bump is valid—no schema change. The application team updates eval_config.yaml to pin to v2.4.0. CI now runs against 1,300 cases. Pass rate drops to 86%, below the threshold. Rather than lowering the threshold, the model team investigates the new safety cases, identifies a prompt gap, and fixes it. This is the system working correctly: harder dataset, same bar, visible signal.
Common Mistakes
Mutable 'latest' pointer — pointing CI to latest instead of a pinned version means a dataset edit can silently change pass rates on already-merged application commits.
Lowering thresholds to unblock deploys — adjusting promotion_rules.min_pass_rate downward to make a failing build pass defeats the purpose of the gate; treat the threshold as a contract, not a dial.
No diff between dataset versions — changing cases without a diff step means no one notices when a critical safety category is accidentally removed from the dataset.
Storing large binaries in Git without LFS — a 500MB JSONL file committed directly to Git bloats the repo permanently and slows all future clones.
Treating ground truth as immutable — expected outputs must be maintained as the product evolves; stale ground truth produces misleading 'regressions' against intentional behavior changes.
Best Practices
Store promotion_rules inside the dataset manifest, not the application CI file—this couples the threshold to the dataset version, preventing them from drifting apart.
Require PR review for MAJOR dataset version bumps; minor and patch bumps can merge automatically if CI passes.
Tag datasets with semantic version plus creation timestamp (evals/v2.3.1+20260710) for easier chronological sorting without losing version semantics.
Cache eval results keyed on (dataset_version, model_id, prompt_hash) to avoid re-running 1,000 LLM calls when only unrelated code changed.
Run a nightly scheduled eval against the main branch with the latest dataset version to catch model API drift independent of application code changes.
Performance and Scalability

For datasets under 500 cases, the entire eval run completes within a few minutes and does not need special optimization. Beyond 1,000 cases, two strategies matter: result caching and parallel execution. A cache keyed on (case_id, model_version, prompt_hash) lets re-runs skip already-scored cases, cutting latency by 80–95% when only a small prompt change is being tested. Parallel execution (covered in a companion topic) shards the case list across multiple workers, each making independent API calls.

DVC remote storage should use content-addressable blob storage (S3, GCS) to avoid duplicating case files across dataset versions when only a small fraction of cases change. DVC's deduplication means that v2.3.1 and v2.4.0 sharing 1,100 identical cases store those cases only once in the remote.
Tooling and Ecosystem
DVC (Data Version Control) — Git-compatible tool for versioning large data files with S3/GCS/Azure backends; the standard choice for JSONL and Parquet eval datasets.
Git LFS — lighter-weight alternative to DVC for datasets that do not need DVC pipeline features; stores large files on GitHub/GitLab's LFS backend.
Weights & Biases (W&B Artifacts) — purpose-built artifact versioning with lineage graphs; integrates with W&B's experiment tracking so dataset version and model run are linked automatically.
MLflow Model Registry — tracks model versions alongside dataset versions; useful when evals are part of a broader ML lifecycle already using MLflow.
Braintrust — managed eval platform with built-in dataset versioning, experiment tracking, and CI integration; reduces the need to build custom promotion gate scripts.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Versioning
CI Integration
Best for
Git + DVC
Full SemVer tags
Custom scripts
Teams already using DVC for ML
Git LFS
Git tags only
Standard Git Actions
Simpler setup, GitHub-hosted datasets
W&B Artifacts
Artifact lineage
W&B SDK in pipeline
Teams using W&B for experiment tracking
Braintrust
Managed versioning
Native CI hooks
Teams wanting managed eval infrastructure
S3 + manual naming
Folder naming only
Manual, error-prone
Not recommended for production gates
Security and Reliability

Restrict write access to the dataset repo — only designated data engineers should be able to push version tags; application engineers read only. This prevents accidental or malicious dataset tampering that would pass gates for a flawed model.
Verify SHA-256 checksums from the manifest against downloaded case files before running evals—ensures the DVC remote has not been tampered with.
Store DVC remote credentials in CI secrets, never in the dataset repo itself. Use short-lived OIDC tokens rather than long-lived access keys where the cloud provider supports it.
Protect eval results from modification after the CI run by uploading them as immutable CI artifacts with content hashes; this creates a tamper-evident audit trail.
Key Takeaways
Eval datasets must be version-controlled artifacts, not mutable shared files; pin the dataset version in the application CI config just as you pin a dependency version.
Use semantic versioning with domain-specific semantics: MAJOR for schema breaks, MINOR for new cases, PATCH for corrections.
Store promotion rules inside the dataset manifest so thresholds and the test cases that enforce them evolve together.
Track large JSONL or Parquet files with DVC or Git LFS; content-addressable storage deduplicates unchanged cases across versions.
A dataset diff step between versions prevents silent coverage drops from category removal and flags breaking schema changes before they reach CI.
Cache eval results keyed on (case_id, model_version, prompt_hash) to avoid redundant LLM API calls on unchanged cases.
Never lower a promotion threshold to unblock a deploy; instead, fix the model or prompt until it meets the existing bar, or formally propose and review a threshold change.
